content.full_text:
KRAMER ASSOCIATES
THIRTY-FIRST FLOOR
1756 BROADWAY
NEW YORK N.Y. 10019

(212) 582-0303


November 26, 1990

Margaret Ellison, Esq.
Senior Counsel
Fair Political Practices Commission
428 J Street, Suite 800
Sacramento, CA 95814

Dear Ms. Ellison:

     I want to thank you for being so generous with your time in discussing my situation on Thanksgiving eve. In accordance with that discussion, I would appreciate a judgment by the Commission as to whether I am a "public official" under Government Code section 82048 and am therefore subject to the provisions of the Political Reform Act of 1974.

     On July 31st, 1990 I signed a contract with the Department of Insurance ("DOI") to serve as executive director of the California Workers' Compensation Rate Study Commission ("the Commission"). While I obviously have neither the expertise nor the authority to interpret California's conflict of interest provisions, my impression is that the question of whether I am covered turns on two issues. First, is the Commission itself subject to the conflicts of interest provisions -- that is, is the Commission a covered agency? Second, assuming it is not, am I nonetheless a covered public official because the agency that officially contracted for my services is the DOI, rather than the Commission? On the assumption that both issues are relevant, I have attempted to set forth below what appear to be the salient facts.

     As to the first issue, I believe it is reasonably clear that the Commission does not have decision making authority as defined by C.C.R., tit. 2, §18700 (a) (1). Its purpose is to examine California's rate setting mechanism and the approaches used by other states and to make recommendations to the Governor, Legislature, and Commissioner of the DOI. Its findings are purely advisory, and it cannot make, compel, or prevent a governmental decision. Irrespective of what the Commission does or does not recommend, the Governor and Legislature are free to act as they choose with respect to California's rate setting mechanism. Since the Commission has never met and thus never acted, its recommendations cannot have been "routinely and regularly followed." Indeed, since the Commission is only requested to make

and will only make one report -- the report due June 30, 1991 -- whatever
legislative action is or is not ultimately taken with respect to its findings will not
be "routine" or "regular" but a one-time response to the findings of a study
commission. Beyond the fact that the Commission lacks a track record which
would permit a determination that its conclusions are regularly and routinely
adopted, I do not believe there is a legislative expectation that the findings of
this particular Commission will be "routinely" adopted.

As to whether I am a covered person under the Political Reform Act, the
statute creating the Commission houses it within the DOI. However, the DOI is
required to provide the Commission with staffing. Consequently, my contract
was drawn with the DOI. It would have been impossible for me to be hired by
the Commission because my early tasks included shaping the budget and
doing other preliminary groundwork that were undertaken before any
commissioners had been appointed. That is, I had to be hired to begin work to
meet the Commission's deadline, and it was unclear that anyone else had the
authority to hire me.

I believe that the statute makes it clear, however, that while the DOI is to
provide "staff and research assistance," the Commission is charged with
undertaking an independent study and reaching independent conclusions.
There is no requirement that the Commission is to report to, consult with, or in
any manner be guided by the DOI. There is no requirement or suggestion that
the Commission should seek the views or approval of the DOI before submitting
its findings.

More important, I believe my contract is clear on the point that my role as
executive director is to serve the Commission, not the DOI. My "task summary"
is as follows: "Serves as Executive Director of the California Workers'
Compensation Rate Study Commission. Responsible for managing the
operations and activities of the commission." The more detailed description of
my "tasks and responsibilities" essentially involves "overseeing production of
such research and background material as the commission may require" (italics
added). In short, I believe it is clear that my responsibilities and obligations are
those I owe the commissioners, and that the only role of the DOI is to assure that
I have performed the tasks required by the Commission.

It would seem that in terms of the conflicts of interest provisions, the only
distinction between me and the members of the Commission is that because I
am salaried and they are not, I have a contractual relationship with the DOI
which they do not have. Otherwise, I am part of the same non-"decision
making" enterprise. While the DOI is acting as the contracting and paying
agent, functionally I am serving the Commission, and the only role for the DOI is
to determine that I have performed my duties to the satisfaction of the
Commission. In fact, my acceptance of the role of executive director was
conditioned on the understanding that the Commission would be an
independent enterprise, and that the commissioners would understand that I
was accountable only to them.

I appreciate your attention to this issue. As you know, we have our first Commission meeting scheduled for December 17th, and I would not want to have any doubts lingering as to my status. I would have contacted you earlier had I been aware of the possible application of these provisions to me.

Again, thanks for your effort, and I look forward to hearing from you.

Sincerely yours,

[signature]
Orin S. Kramer

enc.

sections.question: Whether the requestor is a 'public official' under Government Code section 82048 and therefore subject to the provisions of the Political Reform Act of 1974, specifically: (1) whether the California Workers' Compensation Rate Study Commission is a covered agency subject to conflict of interest provisions, and (2) whether the requestor is nonetheless a covered public official because the contracting agency is the Department of Insurance rather than the Commission itself.
sections.question_synthetic: The requestor seeks FPPC determination of whether he qualifies as a public official subject to the Political Reform Act based on his role as executive director of an advisory study commission, given that his contract is with the Department of Insurance rather than the Commission itself.
sections.parsing_notes: No section headers found; LLM: This is an incoming request/letter seeking FPPC advice, not an FPPC response. No conclusion or formal advice section is present. Document is dated November 26, 1990 and references a prior Thanksgiving conversation with Margaret Ellison of FPPC. The requestor provides detailed factual background and legal analysis in support of his request.

embedding.summary: This is an incoming request letter from an attorney (Orin S.) seeking FPPC advice on whether his position as executive director of the California Workers' Compensation Rate Study Commission makes him a covered public official under the Political Reform Act of 1974.
